metadata.title: UPSC Mains GS Paper 3 — Disaster Management Comprehensive Syllabus
metadata.purpose: Standalone Disaster Management file for MentorsDaily UPSC portal — AI module generation
metadata.note: Disaster Management is GS3 topic linked to internal security. DM Act 2005 and Sendai Framework 2015-2030 are central references.

book_1.title: Internal Security and Disaster Management
book_1.author: Ashok Kumar & Vipul
book_1.usage: Primary theory — DM cycle, hazard types, response mechanisms

book_2.title: Disaster Management Act 2005
book_2.usage: Legal framework — NDMA, SDMA, DDMA structure and powers

book_3.title: Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015-2030

book_4.title: NDMA Guidelines
book_4.usage: Hazard-specific guidelines — floods, earthquakes, cyclones, chemical disasters

book_5.title: Fundamentals of Physical Geography
book_5.usage: Natural hazards — atmospheric, geological, hydrological processes

key_bodies.NDMA: National Disaster Management Authority — PM as chairperson — policy and guidelines
key_bodies.SDMA: State Disaster Management Authority — CM as chairperson — state level
key_bodies.DDMA: District Disaster Management Authority — Collector + elected representative
key_bodies.NDRF: National Disaster Response Force — 16 battalions — rapid response
key_bodies.SDRF: State Disaster Response Force — state-level rapid response
key_bodies.SEC: State Executive Committee — Chief Secretary level

key_provisions: Section 6 — NDMA powers: policies, plans, guidelines

4_priorities: Priority 3: Investing in DRR for resilience
Priority 4: Enhancing disaster preparedness for effective response
7_global_targets: Substantially reduce global disaster mortality by 2030
Reduce number of affected people globally by 2030
Reduce direct disaster economic loss in relation to GDP
Substantially reduce disaster damage to critical infrastructure
Increase number of countries with national/local DRR strategies
Substantially enhance international cooperation to developing countries
Increase availability of multi-hazard early warning systems
sendai_framework.india_role: India championed CDRI — Coalition for Disaster Resilient Infrastructure — 2019

modules.module_name: Disaster Management — Concepts, Framework and Institutional Structure

topics.topic_name: Disaster Management Concepts — Hazard, Vulnerability, Risk and Resilience
topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 17 — DM Concepts; NDMA guidelines introduction
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Physical Geography Ch 7 — Geomorphic Processes; Class 11 Geography Ch 8 — Natural Hazards
key_concepts: Hazard — potentially damaging natural or human-induced event (earthquake, flood, cyclone)
Vulnerability — conditions determining susceptibility to hazard impacts
Exposure — people, assets, systems in hazard zones
Risk = Hazard × Vulnerability × Exposure ÷ Capacity
Disaster — serious disruption of community functioning exceeding local capacity
Disaster vs Emergency — scale and capacity to cope distinction
DRR — Disaster Risk Reduction — ex-ante approach vs ex-post response
DM Cycle — Prevention → Mitigation → Preparedness → Response → Recovery → Rehabilitation
Mitigation — structural (dams, embankments) vs non-structural (zoning, EWS, insurance)
Preparedness — plans, training, stockpiling, exercises
Response — search and rescue, relief, medical
Recovery — restoration of livelihoods, housing, services
Rehabilitation — long-term reconstruction, building back better
Resilience — capacity to absorb shock and recover
Build Back Better (BBB) — post-disaster reconstruction improving on pre-disaster standards
international_frameworks: Sendai Framework 2015-2030 — 4 priorities
Hyogo Framework 2005-2015 — predecessor
case_studies_india: Odisha cyclone preparedness model — zero mortality goal
Uttarakhand floods 2013 — poor DM response vs Kerala 2018 better response

topics.topic_name: DM Act 2005 — Institutional Architecture and Funding Mechanisms
topics.primary_source: Disaster Management Act 2005 — full text; Ashok Kumar Ch 18
key_concepts: DM Act 2005 — enacted after Gujarat earthquake 2001 and Indian Ocean Tsunami 2004
NDMA — National Disaster Management Authority — PM as Chairperson
8 members — Cabinet Minister rank — apex policy body
SDMA — CM Chairperson — state disaster plans and policies
DDMA — District Collector + elected district representative
National Executive Committee (NEC) — Home Secretary heads — operational coordination
State Executive Committee (SEC) — Chief Secretary heads
NDRF — 16 battalions — deployed from CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB, CISF
NDRF specialisations — flood rescue, USAR (Urban Search and Rescue), CBRN
SDRF — State Disaster Response Force — state funding, state deployment
NDMF — National Disaster Mitigation Fund — long-term risk reduction
NDRF (Fund) — National Disaster Response Fund — quick release for response
SDRF (Fund) — State Disaster Response Fund — 75% Central + 25% State funding
Anomaly: NDRF (Force) vs NDRF (Fund) — same abbreviation, different entities
Post-disaster needs assessment — internationally standardised PDNA methodology
international_frameworks: DM Act 2005 — inspired by IDNDR and Hyogo Framework
case_studies_india: NDRF deployment during Uttarakhand floods 2013, Chennai floods 2015, COVID-19 national response

sample_questions: Explain the institutional structure for disaster management under the DM Act 2005. (10 marks)
Distinguish between disaster mitigation and preparedness. Why is pre-disaster investment more cost-effective? (12.5 marks)

modules.module_name: Geological Disasters — Earthquakes, Volcanoes and Tsunamis

topics.topic_name: Earthquakes — Seismic Zones, Risk and Preparedness
topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 19; NDMA Earthquake Guidelines; NCERT Class 11 Geomorphic Processes
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Physical Geography Ch 3 — Interior of the Earth; Ch 5 — Minerals and Rocks
key_concepts: Plate tectonics — Indian plate collision with Eurasian plate — Himalayan seismicity
Seismic zones in India — BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) Zones II-V
Zone V (Very High risk): J&K, Himachal, Uttarakhand, Sikkim, NE states, Andaman
Zone IV (High risk): Jammu region, Gangetic plain, Delhi, North Bihar
Zone III (Moderate): Kerala, Goa, Lakshadweep, Maharashtra parts
Zone II (Low risk): Rest of India — Peninsular shield stable
Richter Scale vs Moment Magnitude Scale (Mw) — Mw now standard
Bhuj earthquake January 26, 2001 — 7.7 Mw — 20,000 dead — trigger for DM Act
Epicentre, focus/hypocenter — surface expression vs origin
P waves, S waves, Love waves, Rayleigh waves — earthquake wave types
Seismograph network — IMD — National Seismological Network (NSN)
Earthquake-resistant construction — IS codes — importance in Zone IV and V
NDMA guidelines — site selection, building codes, retrofit of old buildings
Community awareness — Drop-Cover-Hold — international earthquake preparedness
NDRF USAR — Urban Search and Rescue — specialised earthquake response
government_agencies: BIS — seismic zone mapping and building codes
international_frameworks: UNDRR Sendai — reducing disaster mortality and losses
USGS — international seismic cooperation

topics.topic_name: Tsunamis and Landslides — Early Warning and Prevention
topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 20; INCOIS tsunami warning; NDMA Landslide Guidelines
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Physical Geography Ch 3; Class 7 Geography Ch 3 — Our Changing Earth
key_concepts: Tsunami — seismic sea wave — submarine earthquake, landslide or volcanic eruption
Indian Ocean Tsunami December 26, 2004 — 9.1 Mw Sumatra — 2.3 lakh dead worldwide
India's death toll 2004 — 12,000+ — Tamil Nadu, Andaman, Kerala, Andhra
ITEWS — Indian Tsunami Early Warning System — INCOIS Hyderabad — 2007
INCOIS — Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services — 24/7 monitoring
Pacific Tsunami Warning System — PTWS — global coordination
Tsunameter (Deep-ocean Assessment and Reporting of Tsunamis — DART buoys)
Warning dissemination — SMS, sirens, media, police — last mile challenge
Landslides — rapid mass movement of soil, rock, debris
Landslide-prone states: Uttarakhand, Himachal, J&K, Sikkim, NE states, Kerala, Karnataka
Causes: heavy rainfall, deforestation, construction on slopes, seismic activity
NLRMP — National Landslide Risk Management Policy 2019
GSI — Geological Survey of India — landslide hazard zonation mapping
Bio-engineering — slope stabilisation through vegetation
Kedarnath 2013 — cloudburst + glacial lake outburst flood + landslide — 5,700+ dead
international_frameworks: IOC-UNESCO — Indian Ocean Tsunami Warning System coordination
Sendai Priority 1 — understanding risk
case_studies_india: Kedarnath disaster 2013 — NDRF's largest rescue operation

sample_questions: Discuss India's seismic vulnerability. What measures have been taken to reduce earthquake risk? (10 marks)
Examine the Indian Tsunami Early Warning System. How effective is it in saving lives? (12.5 marks)

topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 21; CWC Flood Management Documents; NDMA Flood Guidelines
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 7 Geography Ch 3 — Our Changing Earth; Class 11 Physical Geography Ch 13 — Water
key_concepts: Flood-prone areas — 12% of India's geographic area — 40 million hectares
Most flood-prone basins: Brahmaputra, Ganga, Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri
Types of floods: riverine, flash floods, coastal, urban flooding
Flash floods — rapid onset — cloudburst, GLOF (Glacial Lake Outburst Flood)
Urban flooding — impervious surfaces, blocked drains — Chennai 2015, Mumbai 2005
CWC — Central Water Commission — flood forecasting, warning dissemination
Flood forecasting stations — 325 stations across India
Embankments (levees) — linear flood protection — failure leads to catastrophic flooding
Flood plain zoning — land use planning to keep floodplains free
Integrated Flood Management — holistic approach beyond structural measures
NDMA Flood Guidelines 2008 — state DM Plans, early warning, evacuation
Role of forests in flood mitigation — watershed management
Bihar and Assam annual floods — governance and structural challenge
NDRF pre-positioning before monsoon — proactive flood response
Community flood preparedness — Odisha model of zero-mortality cyclone/flood response
international_frameworks: Sendai Target C — reduce disaster economic loss
Integrated Flood Management — WMO and GWP

topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 22; IMD Drought Monitoring; NDMA Drought Management
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 10 Geography Ch 3 — Water Resources; Class 9 Economics Ch 4 — Food Security
key_concepts: Drought types: Meteorological (rainfall deficit), Hydrological (groundwater/stream), Agricultural (soil moisture), Socio-economic
India drought-prone area — 16% of territory — 50 million people affected
IMD drought classification — <75% of normal rainfall = drought
ENSO (El Niño-Southern Oscillation) — primary driver of Indian monsoon variability
Drought monitoring — NDVI (Normalised Difference Vegetation Index) via satellite
Soil moisture deficit — SPI (Standardised Precipitation Index) — drought indicator
MGNREGA as drought relief — employment guarantee in distress years
PMFBY — Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana — crop insurance as drought mitigation
National Drought Management Policy 2016 — drought declaration criteria
State governments declare drought — SDRF funds released
Drought-resistant crop varieties — millets, sorghum promotion
Watershed development — Integrated Watershed Management Programme
Groundwater depletion — drought intensifier — ATAL BHUJAL Yojana
Farmer suicides in drought years — Maharashtra, Vidarbha — social impact
Climate change — droughts becoming more intense and frequent — adaptation needed
international_frameworks: UNCCD — UN Convention to Combat Desertification
Sendai Priority 3 — investing in DRR
case_studies_india: Bundelkhand drought — chronic underdevelopment and water stress

sample_questions: Examine the causes and management of floods in India. Why do floods occur every year despite existing infrastructure? (12.5 marks)
Discuss drought management in India. Is the existing framework adequate? (10 marks)

modules.module_name: Meteorological Disasters — Cyclones, Heat Waves and Cold Waves

topics.topic_name: Cyclones — Risk, Early Warning and Response
topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 23; IMD Cyclone Warning; NDMA Cyclone Guidelines
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Physical Geography Ch 10 — Atmospheric Circulation; Class 7 Ch 8 — Winds, Storms and Cyclones
key_concepts: Tropical cyclones — low-pressure systems — warm ocean (>26°C) fuel
India's cyclone exposure — Bay of Bengal + Arabian Sea — 10% global cyclones
Bay of Bengal more active — warmer, shallow, funnel shape
IMD cyclone classification: Depression → Deep Depression → Cyclone → VSCS → ESCS
Cyclone naming — WMO regional committee — India started 2004
IMD Cyclone Warning Division — 5-day track prediction — +48-hour landfall accuracy
CWRC — Cyclone Warning Research Centre, Chennai
Odisha Model — zero-casualty cyclone preparedness — Phailin 2013 vs 1999 Super Cyclone
Odisha 1999 Super Cyclone — 10,000 dead; Phailin 2013 — 0.9 million evacuated, <50 dead
NDRF pre-positioning — before cyclone landfall — rapid response
Storm surge — most deadly effect of tropical cyclone — coastal inundation
Coastal Cyclone Shelters — multi-purpose buildings — Gujarat, Odisha, AP, TN
Amphan 2020 — West Bengal, Odisha — COVID complication in evacuation
Biparjoy 2023 — Gujarat — successful zero-casualty evacuation model
Arabian Sea cyclones increasing — climate change — Oman, Gujarat, Maharashtra threat
government_agencies: State Revenue and Relief Departments — evacuation management
international_frameworks: RSMCS — Regional Specialised Meteorological Centre — WMO designated for North Indian Ocean
Sendai Priority 4 — preparedness
case_studies_india: Odisha cyclone preparedness model — global learning

topics.topic_name: Heat Waves and Cold Waves — India's Emerging Threats
topics.primary_source: IMD Heat Wave Guidelines; NDMA Heat Wave Action Plan; WHO heat health reports
key_concepts: IMD Heat Wave definition — departure of max temp >4.5°C from normal for 2+ days
Severe heat wave — departure >6.4°C — NDMA alert threshold
Heat wave deaths — 25,000+ between 1992-2015 (MHA data)
Andhra Pradesh 2015 heat wave — 2,500 dead — wakeup call for action
Ahmedabad Heat Action Plan 2013 — first city-level heat wave plan in South Asia
Ahmedabad model — WHO collaboration — colour-coded alerts, cooling centres
Urban Heat Island effect — cities 3-5°C hotter than surroundings
Vulnerable groups — outdoor workers, elderly, homeless, slum dwellers
Adaptive cooling — whitewashing roofs, cool roof materials
NDMA Heat Wave Action Plan 2016 — national guideline
Early Warning System — IMD forecast + state response plan trigger
Cold waves — Himachal, J&K, UP, Bihar, Delhi — fog disruption
Cold wave deaths — mostly among homeless, migrant workers, elderly
Night shelter network — urban local bodies — cold wave prevention
Climate change — heat waves becoming more frequent, intense, longer
government_agencies: Urban Local Bodies — cooling centres and shelters
international_frameworks: WHO Heat Health Action Plans — global framework
Lancet Countdown on Climate and Health — heat mortality tracking
case_studies_india: Ahmedabad Heat Action Plan — global model
Andhra Pradesh 2015 heat wave — policy trigger
Delhi fog and cold wave disruption annually

sample_questions: Examine the Odisha model of cyclone preparedness. What lessons can other states learn? (12.5 marks)
Heat waves are becoming India's deadliest disaster. Discuss the measures needed for adaptation. (10 marks)

topics.topic_name: Industrial Disasters — Bhopal Lessons and HAZMAT Management
topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 24; Factories Act 1948; NDMA Industrial Disaster Guidelines
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Chemistry Ch 14 — Biomolecules (toxic chemical basics)
key_concepts: Bhopal Gas Tragedy December 2-3, 1984 — Union Carbide India Ltd — MIC gas leak
Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) — most toxic industrial gas — immediate 3,787 dead, 15,000+ eventual
Lessons: poor site selection (near slums), no community awareness, no emergency plan
SERC (Safety and Environment Resource Centre) — post-Bhopal toxic monitoring
Factories Act 1948 amended — safety provisions for hazardous industries
Environment Protection Act 1986 — enacted after Bhopal — comprehensive environmental law
Public Liability Insurance Act 1991 — mandatory insurance for hazardous industry
CAMEO — Computer-Aided Management of Emergency Operations — US EPA tool
HAZMAT — Hazardous Materials — NDRF specialised HAZMAT response teams
Nuclear disaster — Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) — safety oversight
NDMA Chemical Disaster Guidelines 2007 — industrial accident response
ERDMP — Emergency Response and Disaster Management Plan — mandatory for hazardous units
Mock drills — NDRF HAZMAT training with industry
Petrochemical complex disasters — Vizag gas leak 2020 — styrene vapour — LG Polymers
Chemical disaster zones — industrial clusters like Ankleshwar, Panoli, Naroda
government_agencies: Factory Inspectorates — state level safety inspection
international_frameworks: ILO Convention 174 — Prevention of Major Industrial Accidents
UNEP APELL — Awareness and Preparedness for Emergencies at Local Level
case_studies_india: Bhopal Gas Tragedy 1984 — worst industrial disaster
ONGC well blowout incidents — oil and gas sector

sample_questions: What lessons does the Bhopal Gas Tragedy offer for industrial disaster management in India? (12.5 marks)
Discuss the legal and institutional framework for managing chemical disasters in India. (10 marks)

modules.module_name: Disaster Response — NDRF, Armed Forces and Civil Administration

topics.topic_name: NDRF — Structure, Operations and Coordination with Civil Administration
topics.primary_source: Ashok Kumar Ch 25; NDMA NDRF Deployment SOP; MHA Annual Report
key_concepts: NDRF — 16 battalions — deputed from CRPF (6), BSF (2), CISF (2), ITBP (2), SSB (2), Assam Rifles (2)
NDRF headquarters — New Delhi; 12 locations across India for deployment
Specialised capabilities: Flood rescue, USAR (Urban Search and Rescue), CBRN, landslide
24/7 readiness — pre-positioned before cyclone/flood season
USAR — Urban Search and Rescue — heavy machinery, sniffer dogs, advanced tools
CBRN — Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear — specialised decontamination
Incident Command System (ICS) — unified command structure in field
Unified Command — NDRF + state SDRF + police + district administration
Cluster approach — UN coordination model adopted in India
EOC — Emergency Operations Centre — state and district level — 24x7
IDRN — India Disaster Resource Network — online resource inventory
Community First Responders — trained volunteers — 72-hour window
Armed Forces Disaster Relief — Army, Navy, Air Force — Op Madad activation
Role of IAF — air evacuation, relief dropping in inaccessible areas
Civilian-military coordination SOPs — DM Act provisions
government_agencies: District Administration — first responder and coordination
international_frameworks: INSARAG — International Search and Rescue Advisory Group — USAR standards
Sendai Priority 4 — preparedness and response
case_studies_india: NDRF in Turkey earthquake 2023 — international deployment

sample_questions: Discuss the role and capabilities of the National Disaster Response Force. How can it be made more effective? (10 marks)
Examine the coordination challenges between civilian and military authorities during disaster response. (12.5 marks)

modules.module_name: International Frameworks — Sendai, CDRI and India's Global Role

topics.primary_source: Sendai Framework text; UNDRR India country reports; NDMA Sendai implementation
key_concepts: Sendai Framework 2015-2030 — adopted at 3rd UN World Conference on DRR, Sendai Japan
Replaces Hyogo Framework for Action 2005-2015
Scope — expanded to manmade risks, not just natural
Goal — substantial reduction in disaster risk and losses by 2030
4 Priorities for Action: (1) Understanding risk, (2) Governance, (3) Investment in DRR, (4) Preparedness
7 Global Targets — A to G — mortality, affected people, economic losses, infrastructure, strategies, cooperation, EWS
India's progress — National DRR Plan 2019, State/District DM Plans, EWS expansion
SFDRR monitoring — Sendai Monitor — data submission by countries
Hyogo Framework legacy — lessons: risk governance, local implementation gaps
Sustainable Development Goals linkage — SDG 11 (resilient cities), SDG 13 (climate action)
Paris Agreement + Sendai — climate and disaster risk integration
CDRI — Coalition for Disaster Resilient Infrastructure — India launched 2019 at UNGA
CDRI membership — 40+ countries — building resilient infrastructure standards
India's leadership — disaster resilient infrastructure as development agenda
InDRR — India national DRR plan aligned with Sendai targets
international_frameworks: Sendai Framework 2015-2030 — primary global DRR framework
UNDRR — United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction — oversight
CDRI — Coalition for Disaster Resilient Infrastructure
case_studies_india: India's Sendai Monitor reporting — progress tracking
CDRI's role in island states resilient infrastructure

sample_questions: Discuss the key features of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015-2030. How is India implementing it? (12.5 marks)
What is CDRI? Examine India's role in promoting disaster resilient infrastructure globally. (10 marks)

modules.module_name: Climate Change and Disaster Risk — Emerging Challenges

topics.topic_name: Climate Change — Compound Disasters and Adaptation
topics.primary_source: IPCC AR6 India-relevant findings; IPCC-UNDRR joint report; NDMA climate disaster guidelines
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch 16 — Environmental Issues; Class 11 Geography Ch 12 — World Climate
key_concepts: Climate change amplifying disaster risk — frequency and intensity increasing
Compound events — multiple disasters simultaneously (cyclone + flood, drought + heatwave)
Extreme precipitation events — cloudbursts, 100-year floods becoming 10-year events
Sea level rise — 3.3mm/year globally — coastal and island community risk
Glacial retreat — Himalayan cryosphere — GLOF risk increasing
GLOF — Glacial Lake Outburst Flood — Kedarnath 2013, Chamoli 2021
Chamoli disaster February 2021 — Nanda Devi glacier — flash flood — 200+ dead
Drought-flood extreme oscillation — climate non-linearity
Loss and Damage — climate negotiation concept — compensation for climate disasters
Santiago Network — technology transfer for adaptation — COP26 outcome
Green Climate Fund — financing climate adaptation in developing countries
National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) — 8 National Missions
National Mission for Sustaining the Himalayan Ecosystem (NMSHE) — disaster link
Disaster risk informed development — integrating DRR into development planning
Heat Island mitigation — urban forests, cool roofs, wetlands — city resilience
government_agencies: TERI, IITs — research on climate disaster linkage
international_frameworks: IPCC AR6 — scientific basis
Paris Agreement NDC — mitigation and adaptation
Loss and Damage Fund — COP27-28 outcome
case_studies_india: Chennai 2015 floods — urban climate adaptation failure
Kerala 2018 — extreme rainfall event climate attribution

sample_questions: How is climate change increasing disaster risk in India? Discuss with specific examples. (12.5 marks)
What are compound disasters? How should India's disaster management framework address them? (10 marks)

daily_routine.reading: 1.5 hours — Ashok Kumar primary + NDMA guidelines for specific hazards
daily_routine.current_affairs_integration: 30 minutes — recent disaster events and government response

full_study_plan.current_affairs_note: DM questions are heavily current-affairs driven — study every major disaster response and critique.

output_format.daily_target: topic_id + key acts and bodies to memorise
output_format.disaster_data_card: 3-5 statistics or case study facts for the topic
output_format.current_affairs_link: recent disaster event connecting to today's topic
